name: Austrian trunk: Erzherzogtum Oesterreich → Oesterreich-Ungarn → Republic of Austria
description: Austrian state-formation: from the Archduchy (1282) through Habsburg expansion, the Austrian Empire (1804), the Austro-Hungarian compromise (1867), collapse in WWI, short-lived First Republic, Anschluss (1938), Nazi Germany, and Second Republic (1945). Habsburg monarchs ruled continuously 1282-1918 — one of Europe's longest dynastic holds.
ethical_notes: The 1938 Anschluss is sometimes framed by Austrian narratives as 'occupation' (Opferthese — victim thesis), downplaying enthusiastic Austrian participation in Nazi crimes. Modern Austrian historiography since the 1986 Waldheim affair has increasingly confronted this.

sources.citation: Kann, R. A. 'A History of the Habsburg Empire 1526-1918' (1974)
Judson, P. M. 'The Habsburg Empire: A New History' (2016)

links.description: Archduchy of Austria, granted to Rudolf IV (r. 1358-1365) and formalized by Frederick III (1453). Seat of the Habsburg dynasty from 1282 (Rudolf I's victory over Ottokar II of Bohemia).
Austria-Hungary, established by the Ausgleich (Compromise) of 1867, giving Hungary equal status with Austria under a single Habsburg monarch. Dissolved at the end of WWI (October 1918).

name: Czech/Bohemian trunk: Kralovstvi ceske → Československo → Česko
description: Czech state-formation: the Kingdom of Bohemia (1198-1918) under Přemyslid, Luxembourg, Habsburg monarchs; Czechoslovakia (1918-1993); Czech Republic (1993-present). Hussite wars (1419-1434) and the Defenestration of Prague (1618) are pivotal moments. Under Habsburg rule (1526-1918), Bohemia was constitutionally a distinct kingdom.
ethical_notes: The 1945-1948 expulsion of Sudeten Germans (~3 million people) is sometimes omitted from heroic narratives of post-war Czech state-formation — this was one of the largest forced population transfers in European history. The 1968 Prague Spring and Soviet invasion, Velvet Revolution (1989), and Velvet Divorce (1993) shape modern Czech-Slovak identity.

sources.citation: Agnew, H. L. 'The Czechs and the Lands of the Bohemian Crown' (2004)

links.description: Kingdom of Bohemia, elevated to kingdom 1198 under Ottokar I. Peak under Charles IV (1346-1378), also Holy Roman Emperor, who founded Charles University (1348). Joined Habsburg monarchy 1526 after the Battle of Mohács.

Czechoslovakia, declared independent 28 October 1918 by Masaryk. Democratic interwar, then Munich Agreement (1938) betrayal, Nazi occupation, communist takeover (1948), Prague Spring (1968), Velvet Revolution (1989), Velvet Divorce (1 January 1993).

name: Brandenburg-Prussia-Germany trunk: Mark Brandenburg → Koenigreich Preussen → Weimarer Republik
description: The state-building trunk of modern Germany through the Hohenzollern line: Brandenburg march (1157), elevation to Duchy of Prussia (1525) and Kingdom of Prussia (1701), unification of Germany under Prussia (1871), WWI collapse, Weimar Republic (1919-1933). This is the 'Prussian path' — parallel to the Austrian-led Holy Roman Empire path.
ethical_notes: The 'Prussian path' to German unification was led by Bismarck's realpolitik — three short wars (Denmark 1864, Austria 1866, France 1870-71). The French narrative of Prussian militarism and the British/French 'Germany must be contained' framing shaped two world wars. The division of Germany 1945-1990 and the GDR-FRG split are covered separately.

sources.citation: Clark, C. 'Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia 1600-1947' (2006)
Kershaw, I. 'To Hell and Back: Europe 1914-1949' (2015)

links.description: Margraviate of Brandenburg, founded 1157 by Albert the Bear. Became Electorate in the 14th century, held by Hohenzollern from 1415. Personal union with Duchy of Prussia 1618.

Kingdom of Prussia, proclaimed 18 January 1701 when Frederick III of Brandenburg crowned himself Frederick I 'King in Prussia'. Became a great power under Frederick the Great (r. 1740-1786). Led German unification 1871.

Weimar Republic (1919-1933), Germany's first democratic regime, proclaimed 9 November 1918 after the Kaiser's abdication. Undermined by the Treaty of Versailles, hyperinflation (1923), the Great Depression, and political violence. Hitler appointed Chancellor 30 January 1933.

name: Venetian Republic continuity: Serenìsima Repùblega de Venèsia → Italian unification
description: The Most Serene Republic of Venice (697-1797) was one of Europe's longest-lived republics — 1100 years of continuous oligarchic governance. Controlled a maritime empire in the Adriatic and Eastern Mediterranean. Fell to Napoleon in 1797 (Treaty of Campo Formio). Eventually absorbed into unified Italy (1866).
ethical_notes: Venice is romanticized as a 'merchant republic' but was a rigid oligarchy (after the 1297 Serrata closing of the Great Council, only ~200 families could hold office). Its commercial empire was built on the slave trade (9th-15th c.) and the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople (1204).

sources.citation: Norwich, J. J. 'A History of Venice' (1982)
Lane, F. C. 'Venice: A Maritime Republic' (1973)

links.description: Republic of Venice, traditionally founded 697 with the election of the first doge, Paolo Lucio Anafesto. Maritime-commercial empire across the Mediterranean. 1797 dissolution by Napoleon ended 1100 years of continuous governance.

name: Papal States continuity: Stato Pontificio → Vatican City
description: Temporal power of the Papacy: the Papal States (756-1870), a central Italian territorial polity ruled by the Pope. Dissolved by Italian unification (1870); the Roman Question was settled only by the Lateran Treaty (1929) creating Vatican City. A peculiar polity — religious leadership + secular sovereignty.
ethical_notes: The Papacy's temporal power from 756 (Donation of Pepin) to 1870 involved Inquisitions (Roman 1542, Spanish delegated, Portuguese), participation in the trans-Atlantic slave trade justification (Dum Diversas 1452), and suppression of liberal Italian nationalism. The 1929 Lateran Treaty with Mussolini's Italy preserved Vatican sovereignty — a significant political accommodation.

sources.citation: Chadwick, O. 'A History of the Popes 1830-1914' (1998)
Duffy, E. 'Saints and Sinners: A History of the Popes' (2006)

links.description: Papal States, founded by the Donation of Pepin (756) when the Frankish king granted central Italian territories to the Papacy. Survived 1100+ years until Italian unification took Rome on 20 September 1870 (Breach of Porta Pia).
